evidence: WordSketchApiServer Javadoc explicitly documents the security model: 'the server binds exclusively to the loopback address (127.0.0.1) so it is never exposed on any external network interface. All endpoints are unauthenticated — access control is provided by the host firewall and the loopback-only bind.'

CORS headers are applied via HttpApiUtils.setCorsHeader() which is called by every response-sending method (sendJsonResponse, sendError, sendOptionsResponse, sendBinaryResponse) — no endpoint can bypass the CORS header
POST endpoints consistently enforce a 65 KB body size limit via HttpApiUtils.readBodyWithSizeLimit: CorpusQueryHandlers passes maxBytes=65_536, VisualizationHandlers passes maxBytes=65_536
GET endpoints accept arbitrary-length query parameter values (seed, seeds, lemma, adjective, noun) with no length constraint; a very long seed string is passed to BlackLab without truncation

All routes are wrapped with HttpApiUtils.wrapWithErrorHandling, ensuring that IllegalArgumentException (user input errors) maps to 400 and runtime exceptions to 500 consistently — no endpoint can leak an unhandled exception

CorpusQueryHandlers validates CQL pattern complexity (bracket depth > 20 → 400) in addition to JSON validity and field presence — additional trust-boundary hardening beyond the standard requireParam check
No admin vs. regular endpoint distinction exists — there are no privileged operations or bypass paths that would require asymmetric trust enforcement
The /health endpoint is a simple unauthenticated ping with no access to corpus data — correctly excluded from any validation concern
authorization_consistency.issues_preventing_higher_score: Score is 82 — below 85 — so this field is not required.

strengths: Security model is explicitly documented in WordSketchApiServer Javadoc: loopback-only bind, no auth by design, with a clear note that container deployment would require an additional auth layer
CORS header is applied through a single shared method (setCorsHeader) called inside every response path — impossible to accidentally omit it on a new endpoint
POST endpoints uniformly enforce 65 KB body size limits, JSON validity, required-field presence, and business-logic guards (CQL bracket depth) — no sibling POST endpoint skips these checks
wrapWithErrorHandling wraps every route handler, providing consistent exception→HTTP status mapping across all endpoints without per-handler error handling code
authorization_consistency.issue_character: The sole notable inconsistency is that POST endpoints apply an explicit size gate (65 KB limit) and structured validation while GET endpoints accept unbounded query parameter values — a natural HTTP idiom difference, but one that creates an asymmetric trust boundary between the two groups of endpoints.
authorization_consistency.score_rationale: This is a local research tool explicitly designed as unauthenticated, and the security model is consistently applied: all endpoints are equally unauthenticated, CORS is uniformly set, and error handling is uniformly wrapped. The dimension's rubric concerns (some routes with auth, others without; permission magic strings; asymmetric input validation) are largely non-applicable or cleanly handled. The score sits at 82 rather than 90+ because GET endpoints accept unbounded parameter inputs while POST endpoints have explicit size limits — a minor but real asymmetry in input trust boundaries that a developer adding a new GET endpoint would have no guidance to address.

issues.summary: GET endpoint query parameters (seed, lemma, adjective) have no length limit while POST endpoints enforce 65 KB
evidence: HttpApiUtils.readBodyWithSizeLimit enforces maxBytes for POST body reads, and both CorpusQueryHandlers and VisualizationHandlers call it with 65_536
HttpApiUtils.requireParam returns the raw trimmed string for GET parameters with no length check; a seed value of 50,000 characters passes through to SemanticFieldExplorer unchanged

ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti accepts a comma-separated 'seeds' parameter and splits on commas — a pathologically long seeds list creates unbounded work in parseSeedSet and downstream query execution
Given the loopback-only deployment model this is low severity, but it creates an inconsistency: POST body size is guarded, GET parameter size is not
issues.suggestion: Add a MAX_PARAM_LENGTH constant (e.g. 200 characters) in HttpApiUtils and check it inside requireParam or in a new requireBoundedParam method. Apply it in ExplorationHandlers for seed/seeds/adjective/noun and in SketchHandlers for lemma. This mirrors the body-size discipline already applied to POST endpoints.

root_causes: POST endpoints received more security attention because they carry a request body that is a natural DDoS vector; GET parameters were treated as inherently bounded by URL length limits imposed by clients/proxies — a reasonable assumption for a loopback-only tool but still an inconsistency in the explicit validation model
possible_false_positives: The absence of authentication on all endpoints is not an inconsistency — it is the documented design. The dimension's concern about 'some siblings with auth and others without' does not apply here.
